skill.name: figure-legend-gen
skill.description: Generate standardized, publication-quality figure legends for scientific charts, microscopy images, and experimental data visualizations.
skill.summary: Generate standardized figure legends for scientific charts and graphs.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged 112 patterns, but AI evaluation confirms all are false positives from pattern-matching noise: markdown code fences were misidentified as shell backtick execution, scientific acronyms (SEM, SD, CI) as weak cryptographic algorithms, the word 'sample' as 'Windows SAM database', and security checklist items as actual vulnerabilities. The Python script has no network access, no credential handling, no dynamic code execution, and uses argparse with restricted choices. The only real risk factors are expected filesystem I/O for reading input paths and writing output files, which is the tool's core purpose. Overall risk is low.

low_findings.title: User-controlled filesystem output path
low_findings.description: The script writes output to a user-specified file path via --output argument (scripts/main.py:320). While this is the tool's intended behavior, it means a user running the script can write to any filesystem location their permissions allow.

low_findings.title: Documentation-only external URL reference
low_findings.description: SKILL.md:7 contains a hardcoded URL to the GitHub source repository. This is an attribution link in documentation markup, not executed code. The Python script itself makes no network requests.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown code fences detected as shell backtick execution (FALSE POSITIVE)
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer flagged 53 occurrences of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' across SKILL.md, references/legend_templates.md, and other files. These are all markdown code fences (```markdown, ```bash, ```text) and inline code spans used for documentation formatting. No actual shell backtick execution exists.

dangerous_patterns.title: Scientific acronyms misidentified as weak cryptographic algorithms (FALSE POSITIVE)
dangerous_patterns.description: Numerous 'weak cryptographic algorithm' detections were triggered by scientific abbreviations (SEM = Standard Error of Mean, SD = Standard Deviation, CI = Confidence Interval, WT = Wild Type, KO = Knockout, OE = Overexpression) present in academic writing style guides and template code. None are cryptographic in nature.

dangerous_patterns.title: Template placeholders misidentified as Windows SAM database references (FALSE POSITIVE)
dangerous_patterns.description: The word 'sam' appearing within template parameter placeholders like 'sample_description', 'sample_unit', and 'sample_type' in references/legend_templates.md triggered 'Windows SAM database' detections. These are scientific sample description parameters, not Windows credential storage references.

dangerous_patterns.title: Security checklist item flagged as path traversal (FALSE POSITIVE)
dangerous_patterns.description: SKILL.md:168 contains the security checklist item '- [ ] No unauthorized file system access (../)' which explicitly warns against path traversal. The static analyzer flagged the '../' in the warning text as a path traversal sequence, but this is documentation advising against such patterns.

content.value_statement: Writing figure legends that meet journal standards is time-consuming and error-prone. This skill generates structured, publication-quality figure legends for scientific charts, microscopy images, and experimental data using proven academic templates.
actual_capabilities: Generate structured figure legends for 8 chart types including bar charts, line graphs, scatter plots, box plots, heatmaps, microscopy images, flow cytometry, and western blots
Apply academic formatting standards with proper statistical notation (p-values, SEM, SD, IQR) and journal-specific conventions
Include required legend elements: figure number, brief title, main description, data details, methodology context, and statistical significance markers
Output legends in multiple formats: plain text, markdown, or LaTeX
Support both English and Chinese language output
Auto-detect chart type from image filename when not explicitly specified
limitations: Requires user to provide chart type or use filename-based auto-detection; does not perform computer vision analysis of image content
Generated legends use template defaults for sample sizes and statistical parameters unless the user provides specific values
Cannot automatically extract data values from chart images; all numerical details must be supplied by the user
Journal-specific formatting covers Nature, Cell Press, and Science conventions only; other journal styles may need manual adjustment

use_cases.title: Graduate student preparing a manuscript submission
use_cases.description: A PhD student writing their first paper needs properly formatted figure legends. The skill generates complete legends following journal guidelines, ensuring no required element is missed.

use_cases.title: Principal investigator reviewing a lab manuscript draft
use_cases.description: A PI needs to standardize figure legends across a multi-author paper. The skill enforces consistent structure and statistical notation across all figures in the manuscript.

use_cases.title: Scientific editor checking submission compliance
use_cases.description: A journal editor or reviewer wants to verify that figure legends meet formatting requirements. The skill provides a reference template to check legends against Nature, Cell Press, or Science standards.

prompt_templates.title: Basic bar chart legend
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a figure legend for a bar chart comparing cell viability across three treatment groups. The sample size is n=5 per group and the statistical test used was one-way ANOVA with Tukey's post-hoc test.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick legend for a standard comparison experiment

prompt_templates.title: Microscopy image legend
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a figure legend for a confocal microscopy image of HeLa cells stained with DAPI (nuclei) and phalloidin (actin). Include a 20 μm scale bar and specify the microscope used was a Zeiss LSM 880.
prompt_templates.scenario: Detailed legend for fluorescence microscopy publication figures

prompt_templates.title: Multi-panel figure with statistical details
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a figure legend for a scatter plot showing the correlation between mRNA expression and protein levels across 48 patient samples. Include the Pearson correlation coefficient, p-value, and specify that the solid line represents linear regression.
prompt_templates.scenario: Complex legend requiring specific statistical reporting

prompt_templates.title: Western blot with quantification
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a figure legend for a western blot showing p53 and p21 protein expression in wild-type and knockout cell lines under three doses of doxorubicin treatment. Beta-actin was used as the loading control. Quantification from three independent experiments is shown in the adjacent bar graph.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced legend combining multiple experimental details

output_examples.input: Bar chart comparing cell viability across control, 10μM, and 50μM treatment groups
output_examples.output:
**Figure 1.** Comparison of cell viability across control, 10μM, and 50μM treatment groups

Bar chart showing cell viability in HeLa cells treated with increasing concentrations of compound X for 24 hours. Data are presented as mean ± SEM from n=5 independent experiments. Statistical significance was determined by one-way ANOVA with Tukey's post-hoc test; *p<0.05, **p<0.01, ***p<0.001. Error bars represent standard error of the mean (SEM).

output_examples.input: Confocal microscopy of HeLa cells with DAPI and phalloidin staining
output_examples.output:
**Figure 2.** Immunofluorescence staining of HeLa cell culture

Representative confocal microscopy images of HeLa cells stained with DAPI and phalloidin. Scale bar: 20 μm. Images acquired with Zeiss LSM 880 confocal microscope. DAPI (blue) indicates nuclei.

output_examples.input: Scatter plot of mRNA vs protein expression in 48 patient samples
output_examples.output:
**Figure 3.** Correlation between mRNA expression and protein levels

Scatter plot showing the relationship between mRNA expression and protein levels in 48 patient-derived tumor samples. Each point represents an individual patient sample. n=48. Correlation coefficient (r) = 0.82, p < 0.001. Solid line indicates linear regression fit.

best_practices: Always specify the chart type explicitly with --type when running the script to avoid reliance on filename-based auto-detection
Provide actual experimental values for sample size, statistical tests, and significance thresholds rather than accepting template defaults
Review the generated legend against your target journal's specific author guidelines, as the built-in templates cover Nature, Cell Press, and Science conventions primarily
anti_patterns: Generating legends without specifying the sample size or statistical test, which produces vague legends that journals will reject
Using the microscopy template for non-microscopy images, which will produce inappropriate scale bar and staining descriptions
Accepting the default placeholder values in the final manuscript instead of replacing them with your actual experimental data and measurements

faq.question: What chart types does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports eight chart types: bar charts, line graphs, scatter plots, box plots, heatmaps, microscopy images, flow cytometry plots, and western blots.

faq.question: Can this skill read data directly from my chart image?
faq.answer: No. The skill uses templates for legend structure and helps with proper formatting. You must provide your own data values, sample sizes, and statistical results.

faq.question: Does the skill follow specific journal formatting requirements?
faq.answer: The reference style guide includes conventions for Nature, Cell Press, and Science journals. Check your target journal's specific author guidelines as requirements vary.

faq.question: Can I generate legends in languages other than English?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill supports both English and Chinese language output using the --language flag.

faq.question: What output formats are available?
faq.answer: The skill can produce legends in plain text, markdown, or LaTeX format. Markdown is the default as it works well for most manuscript preparation workflows.

faq.question: Do I need to install any dependencies to use this skill?
faq.answer: The core script only requires Python 3.10 or higher with the standard library modules dataclasses and enum. No external packages are needed for basic legend generation.
